@graph.name: Mercedes-Benz Sprinter 208–316 CDI Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz Sprinter 208–316 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Sprinter engine typically costs £2,000–£6,500 supply only depending on generation. OM602 engines sit at the lower end (£1,800–£4,500), while OM612 units run £2,500–£6,500. Reconditioned engines typically cost £1,200–£2,200 supply only, with total installed costs around £1,800–£2,800. The full Sprinter engine supplied and fitted package can climb to £5,900. Fitting adds £800–£1,500 depending on workshop location. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your van.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz Sprinter 208–316 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine generation your Sprinter has. The OM602 (1995–2000) is renowned for exceptional longevity — often exceeding 450,000 km with proper maintenance. It's a robust cast-iron engine with a chain-driven timing system. The CDI engines (OM611/OM612, 2000–2006) are generally robust but suffer from injector seal failure ('black death') and glow plug issues. Rust and corrosion are the most reported problems overall. Check the Era Map above for your specific van.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz Sprinter 208–316 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: Injector seal failure ('black death') on CDI engines — diesel smell, hissing, carbon buildup; glow plug and relay failures causing hard starting in cold weather; manual gearbox bearing wear causing whining in 5th gear; and severe rust on sills, wheel arches and chassis. Wrong oil specification accelerates injector seal wear.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz Sprinter 208–316 CDI engine, or should I scrap the van?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% Mercedes scrap threshold: if your Sprinter is worth £3,000–£7,000 and a recon engine is £3,500–£5,000 (50–100% of the van's value), assess the overall condition carefully. Well-maintained examples with good bodywork justify replacement spend. Tired examples with significant rust may not. The OM602's legendary durability means a well-maintained engine can outlast the bodywork.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz Sprinter 208–316 CDI engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: 208D–316D (1995–2000) is OM602.940 (2.9L I5, 95 PS); 208–313 CDI (2000–2006) is OM611.939 (2.1L I4, 82–129 PS); 216–416 CDI (2000–2006) is OM612.940 (2.7L I5, 156 PS). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the injector seals ('black death'), or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. Injector seal replacement (£300–£800) is significantly cheaper than a full engine replacement. If caught early, the job is straightforward — replace the copper seal and recut the seat in the head. If ignored, injectors can seize in the head, requiring specialist removal. At the first sign of diesel smell or hissing, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz Sprinter 208–316 CDI Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: No longer available — specialist reconditioning is the route

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market rates. Reconditioned Sprinter engines are available from UK specialists with 12-month warranties. A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,200–£2,200 supply only for standard units, with total installed costs around £1,800–£2,800. The full Mercedes Sprinter engine supplied and fitted package can climb to £5,900. Used OM602 engines are becoming increasingly difficult to find, particularly in Australia. Fitting adds £800–£1,500 depending on workshop location.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Sprinter engine typically takes 10–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The commercial van layout makes engines relatively accessible compared to passenger cars.

cta.label: → Compare Sprinter 208–316 CDI engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Injector Seal Failure — "Black Death" (OM611 & OM612 CDI)
problems.symptoms: Strong diesel smell in the cabin, hissing sounds from the engine, visible black tar-like residue on the engine head, rough running, hard starting. The leaking injector seals cause carbon buildup on the engine head.
problems.typicalMileage: 80,000–150,000 miles — common on high-mileage CDI engines.
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (seal replacement, surface cleaning, sometimes injector re-machining). "If you don't wait too long, these can be sealed with a new copper gasket".
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, injectors can seize in the head.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you smell diesel or hear hissing, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: Injector seal failure — often referred to as "black death" — is one of the most common issues on CDI Sprinters. The leaking seals cause carbon buildup that can eventually seize injectors in the cylinder head. If caught early, the fix is straightforward — replace the copper seals and recut the seats. If you notice a diesel smell in the cabin or hear hissing from the engine, book an inspection immediately. Wrong oil specification (not MB 229.52 or 228.51) accelerates injector seal wear.

problems.title: Glow Plug & Relay Failures (OM602, OM611 & OM612)
problems.symptoms: Hard starting in cold weather, glow plug light staying on longer than expected or flashing intermittently. The engine may crank but fail to start when cold.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — age-related and more common in colder climates.
problems.repairCost: £100–£300 (glow plug replacement, relay replacement). Diagnosis should include resistance testing of plugs and checking the relay/module.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, hard starting will worsen.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: Hard starting in cold weather is frequently linked to failed glow plugs or faulty relays. The OM602 uses pre-chamber glow plugs which can be more expensive than modern units. If the glow plug light stays on longer than expected or flashes, have the system diagnosed promptly. Regular testing can prevent starting issues in winter.

problems.title: Manual Gearbox Bearing Wear (All Engines)
problems.symptoms: Whining noises during acceleration, particularly in 5th gear. The noise is common in high-mileage vans used for long-distance driving or heavily loaded conditions.
problems.typicalMileage: 100,000+ miles — accelerated by heavy loads and long-distance use.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, bearing failure can damage the gearbox casing.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if whining is present.

problems.recommendation: Whining noises during acceleration, particularly in 5th gear, indicate worn gearbox bearings. This is common in high-mileage vans used for long-distance driving or heavily loaded conditions. A full gearbox rebuild is often required; high-quality bearings are recommended for repair. Regular oil changes in the gearbox can extend bearing life.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Sprinter 208–316 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch early — new copper seals are inexpensive
Rare but possible at very high mileage
Full engine replacement required if bottom-end or valvetrain fails

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Sprinter CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * OM602-powered Sprinter with full service history — legendary durability
* CDI engine (OM611/OM612) with documented injector seal replacement
* No diesel smell in the cabin or hissing from the engine — injector seals intact
* Full service history with correct oil specification (MB 229.52 or 228.51)
* Good underbody condition — rust is the most reported issue
avoidIf: * Diesel smell in the cabin or hissing — injector seal failure ("black death")
* Hard starting in cold weather — glow plug or relay issues
* Whining noise in 5th gear — gearbox bearing wear
* Poor service history or incorrect oil specification — accelerates injector seal wear
* Severe rust on sills, wheel arches or chassis — rust is the most reported issue

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The first-generation Sprinter is a rugged commercial workhorse, but age has taken its toll. The OM602 is legendary for durability — often exceeding 450,000 km. The CDI engines (OM611/OM612) are robust but suffer from injector seal "black death" and glow plug issues. Rust is the most commonly reported problem. With the 70% Mercedes scrap threshold, a £3,500–£5,000 reconditioned engine on a Sprinter valued at £3,000–£7,000 is 50–100% of the van's value — assess the overall condition carefully. Well-maintained examples with good bodywork justify replacement spend. Tired examples with significant rust may not.

cta.label: → Compare Sprinter 208–316 CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz Sprinter 208–316 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Diesel Smell — Any strong diesel smell in the cabin? (injector seal "black death")
* Hissing Sound — Listen for hissing from the engine (injector seal failure)
* Cold Start — Does it start easily in cold weather? (glow plug or relay issues)
* Service History — Correct oil specification (MB 229.52 or 228.51) is essential
* Rust Check — Check sills, wheel arches, sliding doors and chassis (most reported issue)
* Gearbox Noise — Any whining in 5th gear? (bearing wear)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for corrosion and emissions-related failures
